25. [REVIEW] Asymptotics of Parameter Estimation

25.1. NonBayesian

25.1.1. Asymptotic Performance Measures

Restrict our attention to parameterized family of distributions where the parameter is identifiable:

Definition 25.1 (Identifiability). A parameter in family indexed by is identifiable if satisfies only when .

One of various notions for how an estimate approaches is consistency:

Definition 25.2 (Consistency). Let for denote a sequence of estimates of a parameter . Then the sequence is weakly consistent if

(25.1)

If we have almost-sure convergence, then the sequence is strongly consistent.

As a stronger requirement:

Definition 25.3 (Asymptotic Normality). A sequence of estimates is asymptotically normal if

(25.2)

Asymptotic normality does not imply asymptotic unbiasedness

An even stronger requirement in the Cramer-Rao sense:

Definition 25.4 (Asymptotic Efficiency). A sequence of estimates is asymptotically efficient if it is asymptotically normal with

(25.3)

Perhaps surprisingly, asymptotic efficiency is not the fundamental limit of a sequence of estimators in terms of MSE (because it can be biased?).

25.1.2. Preliminary Observations

Let’s try to characterize when ML estimates have such properties. Consider the empirical divergence

(25.4)

according to which the ML estimate minimizes.

By SLLN, this converges almost surely to the true divergence. So the minimum is achieved at the true value of the parameter. [TODO] We’d expect the ML estimate to be close to but in some cases it will not due to nonuniform convergence phenomena, developed in the sequel.

25.1.3. ML estimates over finite alphabets

Theorem 25.1 (Consistency of ML Estimates in Finite Families). Consider a model family with finite alphabet and parameter set, and suppose that the pmf is nonzero for all and . Then the ML estimator is weakly consistent.
Proof

Weird bound of the probability you choose a non ML estimator using LLN

□

25.1.4. ML estimates in exponential families

When is continuous, under reasonable conditions the ML estimates can be shown to be asymptotically efficient (Wald’s theorem, see 21.1.5* for consistency and Cramer’s 25.5 in the same section for efficiency). The formal analysis is actually more subtle when is finite. But for exponential families it’s not that bad.

Theorem 25.2 (ML Estimates in Exponential Families).

  1. For an exponential familiy with nonzero fisher information, the ML estimate is strongly consistent.
  2. It’s also asymptotically efficient.

[TODO] prove this john

Example wherethe ML estimate is not consistent: sum of two gaussians where one depends on .

Example where the distribution is simple but the likelihood function is complicated:

25.1.5. Mismatched ML estimation

Now let’s consider the case where the true model lies OUTSIDE the parameterized class.

Theorem 25.3 (Asymptotics of ML Estimates in Insufficiently Rich Families). The ML estimate converges to the reverse I-projection.

25.1.6. On the limitations of asymptotic efficiency

[TODO] Hájek–Le Cam theory for theory of the LHS performane estimators?

(We can interchange limits and expectations when we have uniform integrability, which is the relevant generalization of Lebesgue’s dominated convergence theorem).

Example 25.1 (Hodges super-efficient estimator). Superefficient at a single point θ = 0. It beats the usual estimator there asymptotically, but pays for it by doing worse in shrinking neighborhoods around 0. This shows you can “cheat” pointwise asymptotics, so uniform risk matters (i.e.. worst risk over ).

[TODO] The subtlety is that Hodges’ estimator is not just the dumb constant-zero estimator: it is still asymptotically equivalent to the usual efficient estimator at every fixed θ=0, and even better at θ=0.It is worse at some places but those places move with . So fixed asymptotics miss this.

25.2. Bayesian

We’d want to focus on the limiting behavior of the posterior. But it’s difficult to evaluate due to the partition function (normalizing constant). We discussed using stochastic approximations. But alternatively a class of determinstic approximations based on a generalization of Laplace’s method can be used.

25.2.1. Laplace’s Method

Used to approximate an integral of the form

(25.5)

by taking a second order taylor series expansion at the maximum iwthin that range. I.e. approximating the integrand by a Gaussian distribution with matching peak curvature.

[TODO] Laplace ensures that no probability mass is unaccounted for — the tails really do vanish — which is precisely what was missing from the pointwise shape argument alone.

25.2.2. Posterior Asymptotics

Fact 25.1 (proved in L26?). Under suitable conditions, the posterior tends toward a Gaussian distribution centered at the ML estimate . Specifically for large it behaves like

(25.6)

Define the invertible change of variables

(25.7)

so that the equation becomes

(25.8)

For exponential families, the analysis is doable.

For general families, we get:

Theorem 25.4 (Bernstein-von Mises).

Note in both exponential family and bernstein von mises, case, we are evaluating the posteirior locally (this is hidden in the change of variables).

The full density convergence is deferred to L26.

The implication is that the posteiror is mostly determined by the likelihood and not the prior, and it concentrates around the ML estimate with Gaussian shape.
